role_summary: Albany, Georgia community organizer and mother; participant in the Albany Movement; parent of children who integrated previously all-white Albany public schools; SNCC meeting attendee in Atlanta.
ai_reading: Jones's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 53), with Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92, Claiborne County Mississippi) as the top match and the broader cluster including James Oscar Jones (entry 57), Juadine Henderson (entry 68), and Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96). The novel observation her page surfaces is the parent-as-civil-rights-figure signature: Mary Jones's organizing was conducted through her parental role (sending her children to integrate white schools, attending SNCC meetings in Atlanta to learn movement tactics, navigating racial threats while traveling); the catalog tracks this specifically-maternal mode of civil rights participation less explicitly than the front-line-organizer mode that dominates the historiography, and the embedding catches the maternal-organizing register through her testimony as continuous with the broader rural-Mississippi-and-Georgia women's organizing tradition.
biographical_paragraph: While Mary Jones's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), the external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 85 [src: 1]. Jones was an Albany, Georgia native and community participant in the Albany Movement. She describes a childhood in Albany that included the work she did as a child and her early-school memories; she learned about the civil rights movement by reading the newspaper [src: 1]. She participated in the local Albany Movement organizing across the early 1960s and sent her children to integrate previously all-white Albany public schools, the experience that anchors much of her interview testimony. She attended a SNCC meeting in Atlanta, and on the trip home was stranded and threatened in Marietta, Georgia in a racially hostile encounter she describes in the oral history [src: 1].

sources.title: Mary Jones oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
